observations.value: POST /session with completely invalid credentials (e.g., identifier=wrong@wrong.com, secret=wrongpassword) returns HTTP 200 and sets a valid session cookie (Set-Cookie: session=ok; Path=/), instead of returning 401 Unauthorized. The login endpoint accepts any non-empty username/password combination as valid, bypassing all credential verification. Only empty fields trigger the correct 401 'Invalid credentials.' response.

POST /session with identifier=admin@example.com&secret=password returns HTTP 200 and Set-Cookie: session=ok — this may be a valid test account, but combined with the above, the server appears to authenticate any non-empty credentials, not just known accounts.

POST /session with identifier=user@example.com&secret=password also returns HTTP 200 and Set-Cookie: session=ok, further confirming that credential validation is not being enforced — any non-empty identifier/secret pair is accepted.
